title: Exodus 9
subtitle: Plagues Five through Seven — Livestock, Boils, Hail

sections.header: Verses 1—12 — Plagues Five and Six: Livestock and Boils

heb.paragraph: V.3: The fifth plague — deber, a severe pestilence — kills Egypt's livestock while Israel's are unharmed. In later prophetic literature deber is one of the triad of divine judgment: sword, famine, and plague (Jer 14:12; Ezek 6:11). The progression of plagues follows an intensifying pattern: from nuisances (frogs, gnats) to economic devastation (livestock, hail, locusts) to existential threat (death of firstborn).

V.9-11: The sixth plague — shĕḥîn, festering boils — strikes even the magicians who "could not stand before Moses because of the boils." Their earlier ability to replicate plagues collapses entirely. They are now themselves plague-afflicted, unable to maintain ritual purity for priestly function. The irony deepens: those who served Egypt's gods are marked by the same God those gods cannot resist.

hist.historical: The livestock plague (fifth) and boils plague (sixth) target Egyptian animal cults and the priesthood. Cattle were associated with Hathor and Apis; the destruction of livestock struck at Egypt's economic and religious foundations. Boils on the Egyptian magicians rendered them ritually unclean and unable to perform their temple duties — a devastating blow to the priestly class. The kiln soot Moses throws (v. 10) may deliberately parody an Egyptian purification ritual.
hist.context: Plague five (livestock) is verified by Pharaoh's own inspection (v.7) — he sends to check, finds not one Israelite animal dead, and still hardens his heart. The evidence is direct, personal, and conclusive. The boils (plague six) are the first plague to come without warning and the first to physically afflict the Egyptians themselves (not just their environment). Verse 12 marks the first explicit statement that YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart — previously it was Pharaoh hardening himself.

refs.note: Paul cites Exod 9:16 — "for this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth." The hardening is purposeful and global.
The first bowl judgment: "harmful and painful sores came upon the people" — the boils of plague six as eschatological template.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The fifth plague targets Egyptian livestock — horses, donkeys, camels, cattle, sheep and goats. Each of these had divine associations in Egypt: horses were sacred to Set; cattle to Hathor and Apis; rams to Khnum. The comprehensive livestock plague is a systematic judgment against the animal deities of Egypt. Sarna notes the theological precision: the God of Israel demonstrates sovereignty over the creatures Egypt worships.

Pharaoh's investigation — he sends to find out if Israelite livestock has died — is a significant narrative detail. Sarna notes it shows Pharaoh taking the discrimination claim seriously enough to verify. The investigation confirms: not one Israelite animal died. The evidence is as clear as it can be. His subsequent hardening is therefore not uninformed; it is defiance of verified evidence.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The fifth plague is the most concisely narrated of the ten: announcement, execution, discrimination, verification, hardening — five beats in seven verses. Alter notes the compression accelerates the narrative pace. The plagues are building toward something; the economy of narration mirrors the increasing inevitability of the outcome. Pharaoh's investigation and subsequent hardening is the chapter's emotional peak — evidence examined and defied.

The announcement of the seventh plague contains the book's most explicit statement of the purpose of the hardening: 'For this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.' Alter notes the scope has expanded: the Exodus is not merely about Israel's liberation or Egypt's judgment. It is a global declaration of divine identity. Pharaoh is the instrument through which the name of God becomes known throughout the earth.

Calvin on Pharaoh's investigation: 'Pharaoh was forced against his will to make inquiry, as if he had hoped to have found some alleviation of his condition; but he is again convicted.' The investigation is Calvin's evidence of Pharaoh's bad faith — he looks for a loophole, finds none, and hardens anyway. Evidence cannot convert a heart committed to unbelief. This is Calvin's anthropology: the natural mind is not neutral toward evidence; it is actively hostile to what it does not want to see.

Calvin on being raised up for God's purpose: 'Although Pharaoh does not yield voluntary obedience to God, yet he is compelled to serve him involuntarily.' The sovereignty of God extends even to those who resist it — they serve the divine purpose through their resistance. The name of God is proclaimed through Pharaoh's defiance precisely because the defiance makes the divine response necessary and spectacular.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on dever (pestilence/plague on livestock) discusses whether this is a specific disease or a general divine judgment. The note mentions anthrax as one proposed identification, which would fit the historical period. The note cautions against over-specific identification — the text presents the death as miraculously discriminating and precisely timed, which resists purely naturalistic explanation.

The NET note on Pharaoh investigating Israel's livestock and finding none dead discusses the theological meaning: the plague is not natural disease but targeted divine action. The investigation that confirms the separation between Israel and Egypt is itself an act that should produce belief. That it does not — 'yet his heart was unyielding' — is the definition of hardness.

MacArthur: The LORD will make a distinction between the livestock of Israel and that of Egypt. The covenant community is protected within the same territory being judged -- a picture of the gospel's application to those under the blood.

MacArthur: For the first time the text says the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart rather than Pharaoh hardened his heart. Six times Pharaoh hardens his own heart; then God's judicial hardening confirms what Pharaoh's own choices established.

sections.header: Verses 13—35 — Plague Seven: Hail; Pharaoh’s Hardened Heart

heb.paragraph: V.18-23: The seventh plague — bārād, hail — is announced with a warning: those who fear the word of the LORD (yārēʾ ʾet-devar YHWH) shelter their servants and livestock. Some Egyptian officials respond to the warning — the first Egyptians who "fear the word of the LORD." This small turning is theologically significant: the plagues are not just judgment but invitation. YHWH's self-revelation is an offer of knowledge that anyone can accept.

V.34: Pharaoh "hardened his heart" (wayyakbēd) — using the verb kābed (heavy, to make heavy). The word kābed also means "glory" and "liver" (the seat of emotion in Hebrew thought). Pharaoh's heart becomes heavy with refusal; by contrast Moses will speak of Israel's God being "glorified" (kābed) through the same events (14:4). Pharaoh's heaviness of heart and God's weight of glory are set against each other.

hist.historical: Hailstorms of the magnitude described are rare but not unknown in Upper Egypt. The destruction of flax and barley but not wheat and spelt (v. 31–32) provides an agricultural calendar marker — flax and barley mature earlier (January–February), while wheat and spelt are harvested later (March–April). This detail anchors the plague sequence chronologically and has been cited as evidence of eyewitness reporting.
hist.context: The hail plague is the pivot of the plague sequence — the most explicitly theological, with the global purpose statement (v.16: "that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth"). For the first time, some of Pharaoh's servants "feared the word of the LORD" (v.20) and protected themselves — a mixed multitude is beginning to form even within Egypt. Pharaoh's confession (v.27) and immediate retraction (v.34) confirm the diagnosis: he can acknowledge YHWH's righteousness but cannot submit to it. The truth is received cognitively; it changes nothing volitionally.

refs.note: Paul cites v.16 as the definitive statement of God's sovereign use of Pharaoh: "that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth." The hail plague is the verse Paul builds his doctrine of election on.
Rahab: "we have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red Sea... our hearts melted." The proclamation of YHWH's name in all the earth — promised in Exod 9:16 — has reached Jericho by Josh 2.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The purpose statement — 'I will send all my plagues on your heart' — is unusual. Sarna notes plagues are typically sent on the body or the land; sending them 'on your heart' targets the inner person, the seat of decision-making. The phrase suggests that the entire plague sequence is directed at Pharaoh's will, not merely his resources. The goal is not destruction but revelation — forcing Pharaoh to encounter the reality of who God is.

The hail with fire running through it is uniquely lethal — the combination of ice and lightning was unknown in Egypt, which almost never experiences hail. Sarna notes that this climatic impossibility was itself a theological sign: natural forces are being combined in ways that transcend natural possibility. The Egyptians who feared God's word and took shelter (vv.20–21) had learned to read the signs; those who did not were destroyed.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Pharaoh's confession of sin after the hail is his most elaborate verbal acknowledgement of divine justice. Alter notes the speech is grammatically more complex than his earlier responses — the syntax itself signals greater pressure. But the trajectory of the plague narrative makes clear that verbal complexity does not equal genuine change. Pharaoh's elaborate confessions are inversely proportional to his actual repentance. The more elaborate the words, the more complete the subsequent betrayal.

The chapter ends with a double hardening: 'Pharaoh sinned again and hardened his heart, he and his servants.' The servants are now explicitly complicit. The plague narrative has moved from Pharaoh's individual resistance to a collective hardening of the Egyptian ruling class. The Exodus will eventually harden every dimension of Egyptian power before breaking it.

Calvin on the Egyptians who feared God's word: 'Some, taught by so many punishments, began to bow before the power of God; others remained contumacious... and so it will always be, for God's judgments never find all men equally obedient.' The hail plague produces differentiation within Egypt — a remnant of God-fearers emerges. Calvin reads this as evidence that divine judgment always produces a remnant who respond rightly, even within the nation being judged.

On Pharaoh's confession: 'Although he acknowledges the justice of God and the sin of himself and his people, yet this is not true repentance but only that forced confession which extorted by pain... the ungodly often confess, under chastisement, what they immediately deny as soon as it is removed.' The distinction between pain-produced confession and genuine repentance is Calvin's practical theology of the hardened heart.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'such as has never been in Egypt from the day it was founded until now' explains this is a standard superlative formula in the ancient world (cf. Joel 2:2; Dan 12:1) — it does not require that this specific weather event was literally unprecedented in all Egyptian history but that it was uniquely severe and divinely directed. The formula establishes the plague's extraordinary character.

The NET note on Goshen specifically spared from the hail notes this is the fifth time the narrative has marked Goshen's exemption (plagues 4–7). The accumulation of explicit exemptions serves a theological argument: the precision of divine protection is evidence of divine knowledge and covenantal commitment. The plagues are not scatter-shot; they are targeted.

MacArthur: I have raised you up for this very purpose, that I might show you my power and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth. God declares his purpose directly to Pharaoh. Paul cites this in Romans 9:17.

MacArthur: Pharaoh's confession -- This time I have sinned -- is the closest thing to repentance in the plague narrative. But v.35 shows he hardened his heart again when the plague ended. Circumstantial confession without repentance produces no durable change.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth."NIV"But I have raised you up for this very purpose, that I might show you my power and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth."KJV"And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth."

src.title: Hail in Egypt
src.quote: Hail is rare in Egypt (the Delta receives ~150mm rain annually) but documented. A major hail event would be catastrophic precisely because the infrastructure — crops, animals, people — is not protected against it.
src.note: The hail plague is plausible as a rare but hypernatural meteorological event.

src.title: Rom 9:17
src.quote: Paul's explicit citation of Exod 9:16 in his argument about divine sovereignty — making this plague the exegetical anchor of one of the NT's most important doctrinal passages.
src.note: The hail plague is the most directly theologically significant plague for NT doctrine.

rec.text: Paul makes Exod 9:16 the foundation of his discussion of divine sovereignty and hardening — the plague's purpose-statement becomes the OT anchor of NT election theology.
Pharaoh's false repentance (v.27,34) is for Augustine the paradigm of worldly sorrow vs. godly sorrow (2 Cor 7:10): acknowledging consequences without embracing truth.

rows.label: Livestock and Boils: Plagues Targeting the Body and Economy
rows.text: The fifth plague targets Egypt's livestock specifically and spares Israel's completely. The separation (pala) between Israel and Egypt is now geographically visible. The physical distinction maps the covenant distinction.

rows.label: Hail: Egypt Learns the Earth Belongs to YHWH
rows.text: The worst hailstorm in Egypt's history struck everything in the field. Those who feared YHWH's word brought their livestock inside; those who did not lost them. The plague distinguishes between those who take God's word seriously and those who do not.

hebtext.note: V.3: The fifth plague — deber, a severe pestilence — kills Egypt's livestock while Israel's are unharmed. In later prophetic literature deber is one of the triad of divine judgment: sword, famine, and plague (Jer 14:12; Ezek 6:11). The progression of plagues follows an intensifying pattern: from nuisances (frogs, gnats) to economic devastation (livestock, hail, locusts) to existential threat (death of firstborn).

V.9-11: The sixth plague — shĕḥîn, festering boils — strikes even the magicians who "could not stand before Moses because of the boils." Their earlier ability to replicate plagues collapses entirely. They are now themselves plague-afflicted, unable to maintain ritual purity for priestly function. The irony deepens: those who served Egypt's gods are marked by the same God those gods cannot resist.

V.18-23: The seventh plague — bārād, hail — is announced with a warning: those who fear the word of the LORD (yārēʾ ʾet-devar YHWH) shelter their servants and livestock. Some Egyptian officials respond to the warning — the first Egyptians who "fear the word of the LORD." This small turning is theologically significant: the plagues are not just judgment but invitation. YHWH's self-revelation is an offer of knowledge that anyone can accept.

V.34: Pharaoh "hardened his heart" (wayyakbēd) — using the verb kābed (heavy, to make heavy). The word kābed also means "glory" and "liver" (the seat of emotion in Hebrew thought). Pharaoh's heart becomes heavy with refusal; by contrast Moses will speak of Israel's God being "glorified" (kābed) through the same events (14:4). Pharaoh's heaviness of heart and God's weight of glory are set against each other.

thread.text: Paul cites Exod 9:16 — "for this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth." The hardening is purposeful and global.
The first bowl judgment: "harmful and painful sores came upon the people" — the boils of plague six as eschatological template.

Paul cites v.16 as the definitive statement of God's sovereign use of Pharaoh: "that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth." The hail plague is the verse Paul builds his doctrine of election on.
Rahab: "we have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red Sea... our hearts melted." The proclamation of YHWH's name in all the earth — promised in Exod 9:16 — has reached Jericho by Josh 2.

tx.title: Raised up vs kept alive — Romans 9:17 citation
tx.content: MT reads 'for this reason I have made you stand' (heʿĕmadtîkā). LXX translates as 'you were preserved' (διετηρήθης). But when Paul quotes this in Romans 9:17, he uses a different Greek verb (ἐξήγειρά σε, 'I raised you up'), which is closer to MT's causative sense than the LXX's.
tx.note: Paul's rendering is neither MT nor LXX exactly — it may reflect an independent Greek translation tradition or Paul's own theological rendering. This is one of the most discussed OT quotations in Romans.

debate.title: The Purpose of Pharaoh: Romans 9 and Divine Election

positions.name: Pharaoh as vessel of wrath: individual election/reprobation
positions.argument: Paul's use of Exod 9:16 in Rom 9:17 ('For this very purpose I raised you up') and Rom 9:18 ('he hardens whom he wills') establishes Pharaoh as the paradigm case of God's sovereign election to judgment. God prepared Pharaoh as a vessel of wrath (Rom 9:22) to demonstrate his power and proclaim his name. This is the basis for individual unconditional reprobation in Reformed theology.

positions.name: Pharaoh as corporate/historical example
positions.argument: Paul uses Pharaoh to make a corporate/national point about God's freedom in election history (Israel vs. Egypt, Jacob vs. Esau), not to establish individual predestination to damnation. The hardening is judicial — response to persistent rebellion — not unconditional. God raised Pharaoh to his position of power precisely so the Exodus could produce global proclamation of the divine name.

positions.name: Middle position: compatible agency
positions.argument: Both divine sovereignty and human freedom are real. God raised Pharaoh to his position and uses his choices; Pharaoh genuinely chooses to harden; these are compatible rather than competing realities. The text preserves both without resolution. Paul uses the story to establish divine freedom in election, not to settle the philosophical problem of determinism.

coaching.tip: The livestock plague kills 'all' Egyptian livestock (v. 6), yet Pharaoh still has horses later (14:9). Hebrew 'all' often means 'all kinds of' or 'all exposed.' Reading ancient texts requires genre sensitivity.

'I have raised you up for this very purpose, that I might show you my power' (v. 16). Paul quotes this in Romans 9:17. Pharaoh serves God's purposes even in rebellion. Divine sovereignty encompasses human resistance.
